notes.note: A playful epithet for Atticus, cast as the Homeric commander-in-chief whose orders Cicero leaps to obey. The conceit suits Atticus's habit of issuing literary commissions to his friend.

notes.anchor: roughed out what you had ordered
notes.note: Edolavi, literally "I hewed/planed it into shape," a carpenter's metaphor: Cicero says he hastily drafted the piece Atticus had requested. The image is of rough joinery, not polished work.
The Ides fell on the 13th in most months (the 15th in March, May, July, and October); "the day after" thus dates Dolabella's expected visit by the standard Roman reckoning.
Cicero groans at Dolabella, who came to him for instruction in rhetoric and declamation; the "schoolmaster" jest inverts their roles, since it is the pupil who imposes on the teacher's time.
